plotlines.name: Hadji Murad's Defection and Family Ransom Quest
plotlines.description: Hadji Murad in Tiflis as Vorontsov's guest, increasingly restless about his family held by Shamil, pleading to be sent to Grozny to establish contact and negotiate ransom. His fate becomes a matter of imperial-level decision-making.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Sustains central dramatic question: will HM be able to save his family? Transitions his status from guest to semi-prisoner/tool of empire.

plotlines.name: Hadji Murad Dictates His Life Story to Loris-Melikov
plotlines.description: Over multiple sessions HM narrates his entire backstory: childhood in Tselmess, his mother's song, Khunzakh Khans, the Hamzad massacre, his vengeance, alliance with Klugenau, betrayal by Akhmet Khan, fall from the cliff, and eventual alliance then break with Shamil.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Massive exposition device — delivers all HM's backstory in compressed form. Humanizes him, establishes blood-feud motivations, and gives the imperial dossier its content.

plotlines.name: Hadji Murad's Childhood and Mother's Wound
plotlines.description: HM recalls his mother refusing to wet-nurse another Khan's son, being stabbed by her husband, and her song about the wound. Sensory memories of the aoul, grandfather, the lepeshki smell.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Establishes emotional stake for family rescue arc — his mother is now in Shamil's hands. Poetic/mythic texture; origin of HM's fearlessness after shame.

plotlines.name: The Khunzakh Khans and Hamzad's Massacre
plotlines.description: Recounted arc: the Avar Khans are lured to Hamzad's camp and slaughtered; HM flees in shame; he and brother Osman then assassinate Hamzad in revenge, Osman dies.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Seeds HM's blood debt and his foundational 'shame that made him fearless'. Explains why he cannot join Shamil (blood on Shamil's hands).

plotlines.name: Betrayal by Akhmet Khan and the Cliff Escape
plotlines.description: Akhmet Khan frames HM as a traitor to the Russians; HM is chained, marched toward Temir-Khan-Shura, throws himself off a cliff pulling a soldier with him, survives broken. Klugenau's letters of pardon attempt to win him back.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Explains HM's structural distrust of Russian systems and his earlier defection to Shamil. Epistolary texture via the Klugenau letters.

plotlines.name: Klugenau's Letters Offering Pardon
plotlines.description: Two preserved letters from General Klugenau assuring HM of Russian justice, urging him to return. HM does not believe them and goes to Shamil instead.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Mirrors present action (Vorontsov's new promises) — dramatic irony: Russian officials have promised safety before. Foreshadows tragic outcome.

plotlines.name: Hadji Murad's Rivalry with Shamil
plotlines.description: HM's 'sharpest sword' remark to a succession question provokes Shamil's jealousy; Shamil tries to dispose of him in Tabasaran, confiscates his property, demands his death. HM flees to Vorontsov but leaves his family hostage.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Central antagonism driving both flashback and present action. The family hostage situation is the spine of the remaining film.

plotlines.name: Loris-Melikov's Observation of the Four Murids
plotlines.description: Loris-Melikov visits HM's murids, overhears them argue about Shamil's sainthood, learns Khan Mahoma won money gambling, hears Khanefi's milk-brother origin story. He classifies each man's motives — and grows suspicious of Gamzalo's open hatred of Russians.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Introduces the loyal-circle ensemble; plants seeds of suspicion that spies may be among them; texture of mountaineer culture.

plotlines.name: Suspicion of Hidden Motives (Gamzalo / Spy Fear)
plotlines.description: Loris-Melikov's hunch that HM's surrender might be a ruse is echoed by higher officials and finally voiced by Chernyshov to the Tsar. A distrust-thread that threatens HM's fragile position.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Political/paranoid counter-force to HM's open-hearted pleas; keeps stakes high even inside Russian hospitality.

plotlines.name: Vorontsov's Written Report / Bureaucratic Handling of HM
plotlines.description: Vorontsov's December 20 letter to the Minister of War laying out the HM problem, proposing Grozny, sending Loris-Melikov with a Cossack escort. The dossier that travels up to the Tsar.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Transfers the decision upward; dramatizes empire-as-paperwork. The courier-who-beats-drivers motif frames imperial violence.

plotlines.name: Chernyshov vs. Vorontsov Court Rivalry
plotlines.description: Chernyshov, envious of Vorontsov, tries to spin the HM affair against him, recommending deportation to Central Russia. His plan fails only because the Tsar happens to be in a foul mood.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Shows HM's fate decided by petty aristocratic resentments rather than strategy. Underscores absurd contingency of imperial decisions.

plotlines.name: A Day in the Life of Nicholas I
plotlines.description: Tolstoy's famous satirical digression: Nicholas's masquerade seduction of a young virgin, morning rituals, cruel sentencing of the Polish student Bzhezovski to 12,000 strokes, self-mythologizing, approval of Vorontsov's slow-forest plan, evening ballet and diamond ring.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Anti-imperial moral frame of the novel. Exposes the Tsar's hypocrisy, cruelty, and delusional self-importance as the true source of Caucasian violence. Tolstoy's ideological centerpiece.

plotlines.name: Nicholas I's Caucasus Strategy Decision
plotlines.description: Within the digression, a concrete plot beat: Nicholas approves Vorontsov's handling of HM and orders intensification of forest-felling and raids in Chechnya — sealing HM's operational fate and the war's escalation.
plotlines.dramatic_function: The decision that drives HM's subsequent actions and the war's brutality. Connects micro-arc (HM) to macro-arc (imperial war).

plotlines.name: The Polish Student Bzhezovski's Sentencing
plotlines.description: A Polish medical student who wounded his professor is sentenced by Nicholas to run the gauntlet 12,000 strokes — certain torturous death disguised as mercy because 'we have no capital punishment'.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Miniature parable of tsarist cruelty and hypocrisy; parallels the Caucasus policy in moral structure.

plotlines.name: Bibikov and the Uniate Peasants
plotlines.description: Nicholas approves court-martialing peasants who refuse Orthodoxy and transfers state peasants into serfdom. Bibikov silently complies to protect his career.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Widens indictment from Caucasus to the whole imperial system; shows institutional cowardice.

plotlines.name: Vorontsov's Repeater Watch Gift
plotlines.description: HM proudly displays the repeater watch Vorontsov gave him, calls Vorontsov 'kunak'. A small sustained motif of gift and supposed friendship.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Objectifies HM's belief in Russian friendship; ironic given distrust at the top. Could be a recurring visual/aural motif (the chime).

plotlines.name: Caucasus Slow-Advance / Forest-Felling Policy
plotlines.description: Nicholas re-endorses the Ermolov-Velyaminov slow-advance, forest-destruction, food-supply-destruction policy as if it were his own and orders its intensification.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Establishes the war policy that will frame the remainder of the story and HM's operational value.

notes: Chapters 11-13 are dominated by HM's orally dictated autobiography — one extended embedded flashback that contains several sub-arcs (childhood, Khans massacre, Akhmet Khan betrayal, Shamil alliance/break). For a feature adaptation these can be fused into one compact flashback sequence. Chapter 14 is an epistolary chapter (Vorontsov's letter) that doubles as bureaucratic exposition. Chapter 15 is the famous Nicholas I authorial digression — tagged as both political_framing and authorial_digression; considered by Tolstoy scholars the ideological heart of the novel and should not be cut despite its structural oddness. The Khunzakh/Hamzad ensemble (Umma Khan, Abu Nutsal, Bulach, Khansha, Osman, Aseldar) is a dense fusion candidate in any compression pass.
